# Provenance Guard

A backend service a creative platform plugs into to answer one question honestly:
**was this text written by a human, or generated by AI?** It classifies submitted
text with two independent signals, scores its confidence in a way that can say
"I don't know," returns a plain-language transparency label for readers, lets
creators appeal, and records every decision in a structured audit log.

> **Design north star — asymmetric error cost.** On a writing platform, falsely
> branding a *human's* work as AI is worse than missing some AI text. Every
> decision here leans away from false positives: a high bar to call AI, an
> explicit "uncertain" band, and labels that say *"signals suggest"* rather than
> accuse.

See [planning.md](planning.md) for the full spec, architecture diagram, and the
AI tool plan written before implementation.

# DEMO Video
https://www.loom.com/share/cd2f6698e82d489abe107aeeb716d1d1

---

## Setup

```bash
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/Scripts/activate        # Windows (Git Bash);  .venv\Scripts\activate on cmd
pip install -r requirements.txt

cp .env.example .env                  # then paste your GROQ_API_KEY into .env
python app.py                         # serves on http://127.0.0.1:5000
```

The app runs **without** a Groq key too: it degrades gracefully to the
stylometric signal alone, discounts reported confidence, and flags
`degraded_mode: true` in every response and log entry (it never pretends the LLM
ran). Regenerate the calibration table any time with `python -m scripts.eval`.

---

## Architecture overview — the path a submission takes

```
POST /submit {text, creator_id}
   │
   ▼  rate limiter (10/min;100/day)  ──over limit──► 429
   ▼
   ├─► Signal 1: Groq LLM classifier ──► llm_score (0..1)
   ├─► Signal 2: stylometric heuristics ──► stylo_score (0..1)
   │
   ▼  confidence scoring
   ▼  ai_likelihood = 0.65·llm + 0.35·stylo
   ▼  confidence    = max(p, 1-p)        attribution band (likely_ai / uncertain / likely_human)
   │
   ▼  transparency label (3 variants by band)
   │
   ├─► SQLite: content row (status=classified)
   ├─► SQLite: audit_log entry (scores, signals, timestamp)
   │
   ▼  JSON response {content_id, attribution, confidence, ai_likelihood, signals, label}
```

A submission is rate-checked, run through both signals, fused into one
`ai_likelihood` and a `confidence`, mapped to a reader label, persisted, and
logged — all before the response returns. An appeal (`POST /appeal`) looks up the
`content_id`, flips its status to `under_review`, and logs the appeal next to the
original decision for a human reviewer. Full diagram in [planning.md](planning.md#architecture).

### API

| Method | Route      | Purpose                                                        |
|--------|-----------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| POST   | `/submit` | Classify text. Body: `{text, creator_id}`. Returns verdict + label. |
| POST   | `/appeal` | Contest a decision. Body: `{content_id, creator_reasoning}`. Sets `under_review`. |
| GET    | `/log`    | Recent structured audit entries (`?limit=N`).                  |
| GET    | `/queue`  | Appeal review queue (content currently `under_review`).        |
| GET    | `/health` | Liveness + whether the LLM signal is configured.               |

---

## Detection signals

Two **genuinely independent** signals — one semantic, one structural — so the
combination is more informative than either alone.

### Signal 1 — LLM classifier (Groq `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`)
- **Measures:** holistic semantic & stylistic coherence — does the text *read* as
  human or machine? Captures semantic flatness, generic "assistant" voice,
  suspiciously even argumentation, and lack of lived specificity.
- **Output:** strict JSON `{"ai_likelihood": 0..1, "reasoning": "..."}`, clamped to [0,1].
- **Why:** catches things statistics can't — meaning, voice, naturalness.
- **What it misses (blind spot):** LLM detectors are unreliable and biased. They
  over-flag formal or non-native-English prose, can be fooled by lightly
  paraphrased AI, and are non-deterministic. That's exactly why it isn't trusted
  alone — the stylometric signal keeps it honest.

### Signal 2 — Stylometric heuristics (pure Python)
Four measurable sub-metrics, folded into one `stylo_score`:

| Sub-metric | What it captures | AI tendency |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Burstiness** (CV of sentence lengths) | rhythm/variation | low → AI (uniform) |
| **Lexical diversity** (moving-avg type-token ratio) | vocabulary richness | flat mid-band → AI |
| **Casual markers** (contractions, `?!`, `...`, ALL CAPS) | informal human voice | few → AI |
| **AI cliché phrases** ("it is important to note", "furthermore", "delve"…) | filler/transition tics | many → AI |

- **Output:** `stylo_score` ∈ [0,1] plus a `breakdown` of every raw metric (visible
  in `/submit` and `/log`, so each verdict is explainable).
- **Why:** deterministic, free, explainable, and captures *form* the LLM ignores.
- **What it misses (blind spot):** it's a proxy for form, not authorship. Short
  texts have unstable statistics; formal human writing looks "uniform" and
  over-flags; AI told to "write casually" can defeat it. Not trusted alone either.

**Combination:** `ai_likelihood = 0.65·llm_score + 0.35·stylo_score`. The LLM
gets more weight because it reasons about meaning; stylometry is the structural
check. If the LLM is unavailable, the system uses stylometry alone and discounts
confidence (`degraded_mode: true`). Weights and thresholds live in one place,
[provenance_guard/config.py](provenance_guard/config.py), so code and spec can't drift.

---

## Confidence scoring

The score is a **design decision before a technical one.** I decided first what
`0.5` should mean to a reader — *a coin flip, "we genuinely don't know"* — then
built toward it.

- **`ai_likelihood`** ∈ [0,1] — combined probability the text is AI.
- **`confidence` = `max(ai_likelihood, 1 − ai_likelihood)`** ∈ [0.5, 1.0] — the
  probability mass behind whichever verdict the system leans toward. 0.5 = total
  uncertainty; values toward 1.0 = strong evidence. This is why a 0.51 and a 0.95
  produce different labels: one is an honest shrug, the other a confident call.
- **Bands (asymmetric, conservative about calling AI):**

  | `ai_likelihood` | attribution | label variant |
  |-----------------|-------------|---------------|
  | ≥ 0.70 | `likely_ai` | high-confidence AI |
  | 0.35 – 0.70 | `uncertain` | uncertain |
  | ≤ 0.35 | `likely_human` | high-confidence human |

  The bands are **asymmetric on purpose**: the AI threshold (0.70) sits farther
  above the 0.5 midpoint than the human threshold (0.35) sits below it, widening
  the `uncertain` band on the AI side. Borderline text falls into `uncertain`
  rather than `likely_ai` — protecting human writers from the costliest error.
- **Short-text damping:** below 40 words the stylometric score is pulled toward
  0.5, because the statistics aren't reliable enough to support a confident verdict.

### How I validated it's meaningful

`scripts/eval.py` runs four deliberately chosen inputs (clearly AI, clearly human,
two borderline) and checks the *ordering* and *band placement*, not false
precision. Live two-signal run:

| Input | llm | stylo | ai_likelihood | confidence | attribution |
|-------|-----|-------|---------------|------------|-------------|
| Clearly AI-generated | 0.80 | 0.676 | **0.756** | 0.756 | `likely_ai` |
| Clearly human-written | 0.20 | 0.234 | **0.212** | 0.788 | `likely_human` |
| Borderline: formal human | 0.70 | 0.585 | **0.660** | 0.660 | `uncertain` |
| Borderline: lightly edited AI | 0.40 | 0.429 | **0.410** | 0.590 | `uncertain` |

The clearly-AI and clearly-human texts separate by a wide margin (0.756 vs 0.212),
and **both borderline cases correctly fall into `uncertain`**. Setting the AI
threshold to 0.70 was a direct result of this testing: at 0.65 the formal,
non-native-style economics paragraph (0.660) was branded `likely_ai` — a textbook
false positive — so I raised the bar until that human writer landed in `uncertain`.

> The `llm` column is non-deterministic by nature; re-runs may shift a signal score
> by ±0.05, but band placement is stable. Re-run `python -m scripts.eval` to reproduce.

### Two example submissions with different confidence

- **High-confidence:** clearly AI marketing prose → `ai_likelihood 0.756`,
  **confidence 0.76**, attribution `likely_ai`, the 🤖 AI label.
- **Lower-confidence:** the formal, human-written economics paragraph →
  `ai_likelihood 0.660`, **confidence 0.66**, attribution `uncertain`, the ❓ label.
  A naive 0.5 (or even 0.65) cutoff would have wrongly flagged this human writer as
  AI; the uncertain band catches it instead.

---

## Transparency label

Three variants, keyed off the attribution band. Written for a non-technical
reader: a verdict line, a confidence phrase, and a context sentence — never
accusatory. `{confidence}` is the percentage shown to the reader.

**High-confidence AI** (`likely_ai`):
> 🤖 **Likely AI-generated** — Our analysis suggests this text was probably created with the help of AI (confidence: {confidence}%). This is an automated signal, not a certainty. If you're the creator and disagree, you can appeal this label.

**High-confidence human** (`likely_human`):
> ✍️ **Likely human-written** — Our analysis found no strong signs of AI generation in this text (confidence: {confidence}%). No label is perfect, but this reads as human-written work.

**Uncertain** (`uncertain`):
> ❓ **Authorship unclear** — Our analysis couldn't confidently determine whether this text was written by a human or AI (confidence: {confidence}%). We're showing this honestly rather than guessing. Treat the origin as unverified.

All three are reachable: the four `scripts/eval.py` inputs above produce
`likely_ai`, `likely_human`, and `uncertain` (twice). Example rendered AI label:

> 🤖 **Likely AI-generated** — Our analysis suggests this text was probably created with the help of AI (confidence: 76%). This is an automated signal, not a certainty. If you're the creator and disagree, you can appeal this label.

---

## Appeals workflow

- **Who:** the creator of a classified piece (this backend accepts any valid
  `content_id`; a real platform would gate it to the authenticated author).
- **They provide:** `content_id` + `creator_reasoning` (free text).
- **The system:** validates the `content_id`, flips status `classified → under_review`,
  writes an `appeal` audit entry carrying the reasoning **and a snapshot of the
  original decision** (attribution, confidence, both signal scores), and returns a
  confirmation. No automated re-classification — it routes to a human queue.
- **Reviewer queue:** `GET /queue` lists everything `under_review`; joined to
  `/log` it shows the original verdict + signal scores, the creator's reasoning,
  and timestamps — everything needed to uphold or overturn.

```bash
curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:5000/appeal \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"content_id":"PASTE-ID","creator_reasoning":"I wrote this myself; I am a non-native English speaker and write formally."}'
```

```json
{ "content_id": "5da9921d-...", "status": "under_review",
  "message": "Appeal received. This content has been flagged for human review; ...",
  "original_decision": {"attribution": "likely_ai", "confidence": 0.7565, "ai_likelihood": 0.7565} }
```

---

## Rate limiting

Applied to `POST /submit` via Flask-Limiter (in-memory, per client IP):

```
10 per minute ; 100 per day
```

**Reasoning — modeled on real writing-platform behavior, not arbitrary numbers:**
- A genuine creator submits *their own work*. Even a prolific writer revising and
  re-checking a piece submits a handful of times per minute at most — **10/minute**
  comfortably covers bursty legitimate editing while making scripted flooding
  ineffective.
- **100/day** caps sustained abuse: an adversary probing decision thresholds or
  bulk-classifying content hits a hard ceiling, while no honest individual creator
  realistically classifies 100 distinct pieces in a day.
- The two limits work together: the per-minute limit stops fast floods, the daily
  limit stops slow-drip scraping that stays under the minute limit.
- Per-IP keying means one abuser can't degrade service for everyone. In production
  I'd key on authenticated `creator_id` and back it with Redis instead of memory.

**Evidence** — 12 rapid requests, first 10 pass (`201`), the rest are throttled (`429`):

```
201
201
201
201
201
201
201
201
201
201
429
429
```

The `429` body:
```json
{"error": "Rate limit exceeded.", "limit": "10 per 1 minute", "message": "Too many submissions. Please slow down and try again later."}
```

---

## Audit log

Every classification and every appeal is written to a structured SQLite
`audit_log` table (not `print()`): timestamp, content ID, creator, attribution,
confidence, **both individual signal scores**, status, appeal reasoning, and a
JSON `details` blob with the full signal breakdown. `GET /log` returns it as JSON.

Sample (most recent first — two classifications and an appeal on the first piece;
abbreviated `details` for readability):

```json
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "id": 3, "event_type": "appeal", "content_id": "5da9921d-...",
      "creator_id": "creator-alpha", "timestamp": "2026-06-29T00:52:45.767Z",
      "attribution": "likely_ai", "confidence": 0.7565, "ai_likelihood": 0.7565,
      "llm_score": 0.8, "stylo_score": 0.6757, "status": "under_review",
      "appeal_reasoning": "I wrote this myself for a class assignment. English is my second language...",
      "details": {"original_decision": {"attribution": "likely_ai", "confidence": 0.7565, "llm_score": 0.8, "stylo_score": 0.6757, "classified_at": "2026-06-29T00:52:44.087Z"}}
    },
    {
      "id": 2, "event_type": "classification", "content_id": "79070980-...",
      "creator_id": "creator-bravo", "timestamp": "2026-06-29T00:52:45.655Z",
      "attribution": "likely_human", "confidence": 0.8559, "ai_likelihood": 0.1441,
      "llm_score": 0.1, "stylo_score": 0.2259, "status": "classified",
      "appeal_reasoning": null,
      "details": {"label_variant": "likely_human", "degraded_mode": false, "signals": {"llm": {"reasoning": "Idiosyncratic voice and concrete personal detail suggest human authorship.", "...": "..."}}}
    },
    {
      "id": 1, "event_type": "classification", "content_id": "5da9921d-...",
      "creator_id": "creator-alpha", "timestamp": "2026-06-29T00:52:44.087Z",
      "attribution": "likely_ai", "confidence": 0.7565, "ai_likelihood": 0.7565,
      "llm_score": 0.8, "stylo_score": 0.6757, "status": "classified",
      "appeal_reasoning": null,
      "details": {"label_variant": "likely_ai", "degraded_mode": false, "signals": {"stylometric": {"breakdown": {"ai_phrase_hits": 2, "sentence_length_cv": 0.3793, "mattr": 0.8837, "...": "..."}}}}
    }
  ]
}
```

---

## Known limitations

1. **Formal / non-native-English human writing is the hardest case.** Low
   burstiness, few contractions, and an academic register make the *stylometric*
   signal score it AI-like, and LLM detectors are independently known to bias
   against non-native writers — so both signals can err the same direction. In live
   testing the formal economics paragraph scored `ai_likelihood 0.660`, right at the
   edge. This is a direct consequence of stylometry measuring *form, not
   authorship*. Mitigations: the conservative 0.70 AI threshold pushes it to
   `uncertain` rather than `likely_ai`, and the appeal path exists precisely for the
   creators this misjudges.
2. **Very short or highly repetitive creative text** (a haiku, a refrain-heavy
   poem). Tiny token counts make type-token ratio and burstiness meaningless, and
   intentional repetition reads as machine-like uniformity. Short-text damping
   pulls these toward `uncertain`, but a clever 30-word poem can still be misjudged
   — the statistics simply don't have enough signal.
3. **Lightly-edited AI / mixed authorship** has no correct binary answer; the
   system aims to land it in `uncertain` rather than force false precision.

---

## Spec reflection

- **How the spec helped:** writing the *uncertainty representation* section in
  [planning.md](planning.md) *before* coding forced me to define what `0.5` means
  to a user first, then derive the math. Because `confidence = max(p, 1−p)` and the
  asymmetric bands were pinned down in the spec, the scoring code was a direct
  translation with one source of truth ([config.py](provenance_guard/config.py)) —
  no mid-build agonizing over "what should 0.66 mean?"
- **How the implementation diverged:** two ways. (1) The spec didn't anticipate
  running *without* a Groq key, so during M3 I added a **graceful degraded mode**
  (stylometry-only, discounted confidence, `degraded_mode` surfaced everywhere) —
  a production-safety concern the spec missed. (2) The spec fixed the AI threshold
  at 0.65, but live testing showed that branded a formal human writer as AI, so I
  raised it to **0.70** and updated planning.md to match — the empirical run
  overrode the a-priori guess, which is the whole point of calibration.

---

## AI usage

1. **Stylometric signal design.** I directed the AI to draft a pure-Python
   stylometric scorer from my planning.md sub-metric list (burstiness, TTR, casual
   markers, AI phrases). It produced a plain type-token ratio; I **overrode** it
   with a length-robust moving-average TTR (MATTR) because raw TTR mechanically
   falls as text grows and would have penalized longer human pieces. I also
   re-tuned the sub-metric weights after the first calibration run flagged formal
   human prose too aggressively.
2. **Confidence combiner.** I asked the AI to implement the signal-fusion function
   from my *uncertainty representation* spec. The first version used a symmetric
   0.5 cutoff between AI and human; I **corrected** it to the asymmetric 0.35/0.70
   bands so borderline text routes to `uncertain`, and centralized the weights and
   thresholds in `config.py` so the code and spec can't silently drift apart.

---

## Walkthrough

A short portfolio walkthrough video accompanies this submission: a quick tour
showing `/submit`, the three label variants, an appeal updating status, the audit
log, and rate limiting in action, with commentary on the asymmetric-error design.
The detailed evidence lives in this README and the source code.
